claim: 93% of lost dogs and 74% of lost cats are eventually recovered, based on a nationally representative US survey of 1,015 pet-owning households.
stance_target: high recovery rates for dogs confirm that active search is worth sustained effort
scope_conditions: random-digit-dial telephone survey, September–November 2010, 1,015 US households
quoted_support: 85 percent of those lost dogs and cats were recovered; The percentage of lost dogs compared to lost cats was quite similar – 14 percent for dogs and 15 percent for cats; and Cat guardians were less likely to find their cat – only 74 percent of lost cats were recovered, while 93 percent of lost dogs were recovered.
numbers: 15% of households lost a dog or cat in 5 years

claim: 49% of dogs are found by the owner searching the neighborhood; ID tags or microchips account for 15% of recoveries; only 6% are found at shelters.
stance_target: the dominant recovery pathway is owner-led local physical search, not shelter intake — Nona search teams should prioritise local canvassing over institutional channels
quoted_support: Forty-nine percent of dog guardians found their dog by searching the neighborhood, and 15 percent of the dogs were recovered because they were wearing an ID tag or had a micro-chip... Only 6 percent of dog guardians and 2 percent of cat guardians found their lost pets at a shelter.
